title: Format & Validate Contacts
description: Use string methods to format contact display output and validate email addresses. Create format_contact() that returns a neatly aligned string, and validate_email() that checks for basic email structure.
starter_code:
# Contact Manager — Step 4: Format & Validate
# Use string methods to produce clean output and validate data.

contacts = [
    {'name': 'Alice', 'phone': '555-0001', 'email': 'alice@example.com'},
    {'name': 'Bob', 'phone': '555-0002', 'email': 'bob@example.com'},
    {'name': 'Charlie', 'phone': '555-0003', 'email': 'charlie@example.com'},
]

def format_contact(contact):
    """Return a formatted string like: 'Alice          555-0001   alice@example.com'
    Name should be left-justified in a 15-character field.
    Phone should be left-justified in a 15-character field.
    """
    # Your code here — use string ljust() or format()
    return ''

def validate_email(email):
    """Return True if email contains exactly one '@' with text on both sides."""
    # Your code here
    return False

# Test it
for c in contacts:
    print(format_contact(c))

print(f"\nValid: {validate_email('alice@example.com')}")
print(f"Invalid: {validate_email('not-an-email')}")
test_code:
result = format_contact({'name': 'Alice', 'phone': '555-0001', 'email': 'alice@example.com'})
assert isinstance(result, str), 'format_contact should return a string'
assert 'Alice' in result, 'Formatted string should contain the name'
assert '555-0001' in result, 'Formatted string should contain the phone'
assert 'alice@example.com' in result, 'Formatted string should contain the email'
r1 = format_contact({'name': 'Al', 'phone': '1', 'email': 'a@b'})
r2 = format_contact({'name': 'Alexander', 'phone': '555-1234', 'email': 'alex@test.com'})
assert r1.index('1') == r2.index('555-1234'), 'Phone column should start at the same position (name is padded)'
assert validate_email('alice@example.com') is True, 'alice@example.com should be valid'
assert validate_email('user@domain.org') is True, 'user@domain.org should be valid'
assert validate_email('not-an-email') is False, 'no @ sign should be invalid'
assert validate_email('@missing-name.com') is False, 'nothing before @ should be invalid'
assert validate_email('missing-domain@') is False, 'nothing after @ should be invalid'
assert validate_email('two@@ats.com') is False, 'two @ signs should be invalid'
acceptance_criteria: format_contact() returns a string with name, phone, email
Output is fixed-width (aligned columns regardless of data length)
validate_email() returns True for valid emails (has @ with text on both sides)
validate_email() returns False for missing @, empty sides, or multiple @
hints: Use 'Alice'.ljust(15) to left-justify in a 15-char field
For validate_email: use email.count('@') == 1 to check for exactly one @
Then use email.split('@') and check both parts are non-empty
